Document [15] (Title: Lysosomal Storage Disease -- Evaluation) A patient clinically suspected of having an LSD, but with normal enzyme levels and no mutation in the enzyme coding gene should be further investigated. Such patients can have a mutation in the gene for the enzyme activator. A protein called prosaposin (PSAP) has four subunits A, B, C, and D. A gene codes for prosaposin. The code for each subunit occupies separate points on the gene. The prosaposin protein is cleaved into its subunit components. Each subunit functions as an activator protein for a specific enzyme. In the absence of its activator, the normal enzyme is rendered functionless. The substrate accumulates and may leak into the urine; examples are MLD (saposin B) and Gaucher disease (saposin C). Document [22] (Title: Biochemistry, Protein Targeting and I Cell Diseases -- Clinical Significance -- Other Disorders of Protein Targeting) Neurofibrillary tangles and amyloid plaques characterize Alzheimer disease. The main component of these plaques is beta-amyloid. Beta-amyloid is formed by the beta-cleavage of amyloid precursor protein (APP) by beta-site APP-cleaving enzyme (BACE). [88] FRET (fluorescence resonance energy transfer) studies have revealed that APP and BACE, a secretase, localize mainly in the early endosome, meaning that the early endosome is the area of cleavage. [88] [89] Other studies have shown that blocking proteolysis with protease inhibitors or removing gamma-secretase using a presenilin knock-out leads to APP collection within the lysosomes. [89] The studies done by Tam et al showed that both gamma-secretase and beta-secretase work at an acidic pH in the lysosomes. [89] This explains a possible therapeutic benefit of using chloroquine, an antimalarial, in Alzheimer disease. Chloroquine increases the pH in endosomes and lysosomes, disrupting APP's cleavage and inhibiting beta-amyloid formation. This leads to the accumulation of APP in lysosomes. Contrarily, with the Swedish mutation (APPsw), the rate of beta-cleavage is increased so much that marked fluorescent APP cannot be visualized in the lysosomes. Beta-amyloid plaques can disrupt synapses and membranes, leading to lysosomal rupture and causing cell death. [89]

Document [27] (Title: Biochemistry, Protein Targeting and I Cell Diseases -- Molecular Level -- Transport of Lysosomal Proteins (Enzymes)) Lysosomal integral membrane protein type 2 (LIMP-2/SCARB2) is the MPR-independent trafficking receptor responsible for beta-glucocerebrosidase transport. [40] The pH-dependent cargo (beta-glucocerebrosidase) is bound to LIMP-2 in the RER. The complex travels across the cis- Golgi to the trans -Golgi and is taken to the lysosome, where the acidic pH disconnects the complex. [40] LIMP types I, III,10, 12, and 39 also cross the Golgi in an anterograde fashion. The lack of involvement of beta-glucocerebrosidase in the M6P-dependent trafficking pathway accounts for its normal serum levels in ICD. The identification that LIMPs can transport to lysosomes in the presence of tunicamycin, a drug that blocks the traditional transport of hydrolases, further adds evidence that LIMP transport is not coupled with that of soluble hydrolases and to the fact of the existence of an MPR-independent pathway. [41] Cathepsin D, alpha-glucosidase, and acid phosphatase are other enzymes in this less-used delivery system. [42] Sortilin is another receptor that can bind several targets, such as neurotensin and receptor-associated proteins, via an M6P-independent route. It can perform Golgi to lysosome transport of prosaposin, GM2 activator protein, acid sphingomyelinase, cathepsin D, and cathepsin H. [31] Though some are detailed, the exact mechanisms of how each lysosomal enzyme is delivered via the M6P-independent trafficking pathway are yet to be fully explored. [39]
Document [28] (Title: Biochemistry, Protein Targeting and I Cell Diseases -- Cellular Level -- Clathrin) Clathrin-coated vesicles are essential for lysosome biogenesis, endocytosis, retrograde transport (endosome-to-TGN), and anterograde transport (TGN-to-endosomes). [19] [26] Their sizes vary from 50 to 80 nm. The structure of clathrin is described as a 'triskelion' due to the combination of 3 heavy chains that each associate with a light chain being approximately 190 kDa and 25 kDa, respectively. Having a pH below 7.0 promotes clathrin cage formation. [27] Clathrin-coated vesicles contain high amounts of MPR (mannose-6-phosphate receptors). Clathrin-coated vesicles almost entirely populate the TGN, with COPI vesicles also present to a lesser extent. Clathrin adaptor proteins can be either monomeric or tetrameric. Clathrin adaptor protein 1 (AP1), a heterotetrameric protein, functions to form vesicles at the TGN, sort lysosomal hydrolases, and retrieve cargo like SNAREs, MPRs, sortilin, and furin. [10] AP2, which forms at the cell membrane, mediates the clathrin-dependent endocytosis. AP3 may directly transport proteins from the TGN to lysosomes, but the function of AP4 has not been clearly defined. [28] The externalized LDL (low-density lipoprotein) receptor takes LDL into the cell via clathrin-mediated endocytosis. Once LDL binds to its receptor, a clathrin-coated pit forms, which intracellularly moves the LDL receptor-ligand complex. Clathrin dissociates from these vesicles and then returns to the plasma membrane for further use. [29] [30] Dynamin, a GTPase, plays a role in the scission of clathrin-coated vesicles. [17]

Document [15] (Title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1993) Protease inhibitors are a class of direct‑acting antiviral agents that target viral protease enzymes essential for the replication of certain viruses, most notably HIV. Key members of this class include fosamprenavir, indinavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir, each of which is classified as a protease inhibitor. By inhibiting the protease step in the viral life cycle, these drugs prevent the maturation of infectious viral particles and are integral components of combination antiretroviral therapy used to achieve viral suppression in patients with HIV infection. Understanding that fosamprenavir, indinavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir all belong to the protease inhibitor group helps clinicians select appropriate regimens, anticipate class‑related effects, and manage drug‑drug interactions within comprehensive HIV care.

The “inhibitory proteas” concept itself is classified under the proteasome inhibitor ixazomib, and the peptide‑derived compound hirudiny is identified as an instance of this inhibitory proteas class. Together, these relationships highlight a network in which several antiviral protease inhibitors intersect with proteasome‑targeting mechanisms, suggesting potential overlapping therapeutic effects or shared molecular targets relevant to antiviral and anticancer drug development.
Document [29] (Title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 2 -- Community 561) Protease inhibitors that are classified as direct‑acting antivirals encompass several agents, including fosamprenavir, amprenavir, atazanavir and tipranavir. These drugs are linked to the broader class of inhibitory proteas, a pharmacologic group that also contains compounds such as hirudiny and the proteasome inhibitor ixazomib. Within this hierarchy, amprenavir is specifically noted as a child member of the inhibitory proteas category. In terms of drug‑interaction safety, cisapride monohydrate is contraindicated for use together with amprenavir, as well as with the diuretic furosemide and the antifungal fluconazole. Recognizing these class relationships and contraindications is essential for appropriate prescribing and for avoiding adverse drug interactions.
Document [30] (Title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2310) Fosamprenavir, indinavir, and tipranavir are all members of the protease inhibitor class, which constitutes a group of direct‑acting antiviral agents. As protease inhibitors, each drug targets viral protease enzymes, thereby blocking the proteolytic processing of viral polyproteins that is essential for viral replication. This mechanism makes them key components of antiretroviral therapy, particularly in the management of HIV infection, where inhibition of the HIV protease enzyme reduces viral load and slows disease progression. Recognizing that fosamprenavir, indinavir, and tipranavir share this common pharmacologic class helps clinicians select appropriate regimen components and understand their collective role in disrupting viral life cycles.

Alprazolam exhibits overlapping contraindications with itraconazole and indinavir, reflecting the same CYP3A4‑mediated interaction pathway. Lovastatin, a lipid‑lowering agent, is likewise contraindicated with the same protease inhibitors and antifungal agents (atazanavir, darunavir, dasabuvir, fosamprenavir, indinavir, itraconazole, kobicistat, ombitasvir, paritaprevir, tipranavir) and additionally with voriconazole, saquinavir mesylate, and other CYP3A4 inhibitors; co‑administration can markedly increase plasma concentrations of alfuzosin or lovastatin, leading to hypotension, severe myopathy, or rhabdomyolysis. Protease inhibitors that are direct‑acting antivirals—fosamprenavir, amprenavir, indinavir, atazanavir and tipranavir—are grouped within the broader inhibitory proteas (proteasome inhibitor) category, indicating potential effects on proteasome activity.
